stem:
You are the obstetric medicine registrar reviewing a 35-year-old G2P1 woman at 32+0 weeks gestation in the joint obstetric-endocrine clinic. She was diagnosed with gestational diabetes at 26 weeks on a 75g OGTT (fasting 5.3, 1 hour 11.2, 2 hour 9.4 mmol/L). She has been on dietary modification plus metformin 1 g twice daily for the last four weeks. She is otherwise well; her BMI at booking was 31 kg/m2 and her previous pregnancy was complicated by a 4.1 kg neonate at term without operative delivery.

Her blood glucose monitoring diary over the last 14 days shows fasting capillary glucoses ranging 5.6 to 6.1 mmol/L (target <5.1), one hour postprandial values ranging 7.5 to 8.4 mmol/L (target <7.4), and she reports good dietary adherence with a low GI breakfast. Her HbA1c last week was 5.8%. A growth ultrasound today shows estimated foetal weight on the 92nd centile with a normal abdominal circumference to head circumference ratio.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 82/min, BP 122/76 mmHg, RR 16/min, SpO2 99% room air, temperature 36.7 °C, foetal heart 140/min
data_table.BGL diary: Fasting 5.6 to 6.1 mmol/L (target <5.1); 1 hour postprandial 7.5 to 8.4 mmol/L (target <7.4)
data_table.Investigations: HbA1c 5.8%; EFW 92nd centile; AC:HC ratio normal; amniotic fluid index 18 cm

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Add bedtime intermediate-acting insulin isophane 8 units and continue metformin
options.rationale: Persistent fasting hyperglycaemia above target on maximal metformin requires addition of basal insulin (typically isophane at bedtime); this targets overnight gluconeogenesis specifically and is the ADIPS-endorsed escalation. (Source: ADIPS Consensus Guidelines GDM; RANZCOG Diabetes in Pregnancy)

options.text: Increase metformin to 1.5 g twice daily and reassess in two weeks
options.rationale: Maximum daily metformin dose in pregnancy is 2 g/day (per ADIPS); she is already at maximum, and ongoing hyperglycaemia mandates insulin addition. (Source: ADIPS Consensus Guidelines GDM)

options.text: Switch from metformin to glibenclamide 5 mg twice daily as an alternative oral agent
options.rationale: Glibenclamide crosses the placenta, carries higher neonatal hypoglycaemia and macrosomia rates than insulin, and is not first-line in Australia. (Source: ADIPS Consensus Guidelines GDM)

options.text: Plan elective induction of labour at 37 weeks for foetal macrosomia prophylaxis
options.rationale: Induction discussion belongs at 38 to 39 weeks for diet/metformin GDM; current management is to first achieve glycaemic targets with insulin escalation. (Source: RANZCOG Diabetes in Pregnancy)

options.text: Continue current management as her HbA1c of 5.8% indicates adequate overall control
options.rationale: HbA1c in late pregnancy is unreliable due to red cell turnover and underestimates postprandial peaks; capillary targets, not HbA1c, govern treatment escalation. (Source: ADIPS Consensus Guidelines GDM)

explanation.summary: GDM in Australia is treated to capillary targets of fasting <5.1 mmol/L and either 1 hour postprandial <7.4 mmol/L or 2 hour postprandial <6.7 mmol/L. First line is medical nutrition therapy plus exercise; second line is metformin (max 2 g/day in pregnancy); third line is insulin. Insulin is added rather than substituted for metformin: basal isophane targets fasting glucose, rapid analogues target postprandial peaks. HbA1c is unreliable in late pregnancy. Foetal growth above 90th centile with disproportionate abdominal circumference signals foetal hyperinsulinism and is an additional indication for tighter control.
key_points: Capillary targets: fasting <5.1, 1 hour <7.4, 2 hour <6.7 mmol/L
Maximum metformin in pregnancy 2 g/day
Add basal insulin (isophane) rather than swap, for fasting hyperglycaemia
HbA1c unreliable in pregnancy; use capillary diary
explanation.pearls: Persistent fasting hyperglycaemia on maximum metformin in GDM is best addressed by adding bedtime isophane targeting overnight gluconeogenesis; switching to glibenclamide is not Australian first-line practice.

stem:
You are the postnatal ward registrar reviewing a 36-year-old G3P3 woman who delivered 8 hours ago by emergency lower segment caesarean at 38+5 weeks for failure to progress after a 14-hour labour. The procedure was uncomplicated; estimated blood loss was 750 mL. Her booking BMI was 34 kg/m2. Her mother had an unprovoked DVT at age 55 and her sister had a pulmonary embolism on the combined oral contraceptive pill in her twenties. She is otherwise well, mobilising with assistance, and the urinary catheter has been removed.

A thrombophilia screen taken at booking by her general practitioner before this admission showed her to be heterozygous for the factor V Leiden mutation. She is not breastfeeding by choice. Today's full blood count is normal, renal function is preserved (creatinine 68 micromol/L, eGFR >90), and there is no active bleeding from her wound or her vagina. The midwife asks you to prescribe thromboprophylaxis.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 84/min, BP 118/72 mmHg, RR 16/min, SpO2 99% room air, temperature 36.7 °C
data_table.Examination: Comfortable, mobilising with assistance; dry wound; lochia normal; calves soft and non tender
data_table.Investigations: Full blood count within normal limits; creatinine 68 micromol/L; eGFR >90 mL/min; factor V Leiden heterozygous

lead_in: What is the most appropriate thromboprophylaxis prescription?

options.text: Enoxaparin 40 mg subcutaneously daily for 7 days only, then mechanical prophylaxis
options.rationale: Seven days covers the immediate caesarean risk, but factor V Leiden plus family history extends the high-risk window; current Australian guidelines extend pharmacological prophylaxis to 6 weeks. (Source: RANZCOG Venous Thromboembolism in Pregnancy and Postpartum)
source_refs: RANZCOG Venous Thromboembolism in Pregnancy and Postpartum

options.text: Subcutaneous enoxaparin 40 mg daily for 6 weeks plus graduated compression stockings
options.rationale: She has multiple postpartum VTE risk factors (caesarean, BMI >30, age >35, low-risk thrombophilia, family history) totalling >=4 RANZCOG/RCOG risk points, which warrants prophylactic LMWH for 6 weeks postpartum plus mechanical prophylaxis. (Source: RANZCOG Venous Thromboembolism in Pregnancy and Postpartum; RCOG Green-top 37a)
source_refs: RANZCOG Venous Thromboembolism in Pregnancy and Postpartum

options.text: Graduated compression stockings alone for 6 weeks without pharmacological prophylaxis
options.rationale: Mechanical prophylaxis alone is inadequate when the risk score reaches the LMWH threshold; this approach underdoses her postpartum risk. (Source: RANZCOG Venous Thromboembolism in Pregnancy and Postpartum)
source_refs: RANZCOG Venous Thromboembolism in Pregnancy and Postpartum

options.text: Aspirin 100 mg orally daily for 6 weeks for venous thromboembolism prevention
options.rationale: Aspirin has limited evidence in obstetric VTE prevention and is not the recommended agent for postpartum prophylaxis; LMWH is the standard. (Source: RANZCOG Venous Thromboembolism in Pregnancy and Postpartum)
source_refs: RANZCOG Venous Thromboembolism in Pregnancy and Postpartum

options.text: Therapeutic enoxaparin 1 mg/kg twice daily for 3 months given factor V Leiden
options.rationale: Treatment doses are reserved for proven VTE or very high risk profiles (previous VTE, antiphospholipid syndrome, homozygous mutations); heterozygous factor V Leiden without prior VTE warrants prophylactic not therapeutic dosing. (Source: RANZCOG Venous Thromboembolism in Pregnancy and Postpartum)
source_refs: RANZCOG Venous Thromboembolism in Pregnancy and Postpartum

explanation.summary: Postpartum VTE risk is stratified using the RANZCOG and RCOG risk scoring systems. Major factors (previous VTE, antiphospholipid syndrome, homozygous thrombophilia) attract treatment-dose LMWH and 6 weeks duration. Multiple intermediate factors (caesarean, BMI >30, age >35, parity >3, smoking, low-risk thrombophilia, prolonged labour, postpartum haemorrhage >1L, family history) attract prophylactic LMWH for variable durations of 10 days to 6 weeks depending on score. Enoxaparin 40 mg subcutaneously daily is the standard prophylactic dose; weight-adjusted dosing (60 mg if 90-130 kg) applies in obesity. Graduated compression stockings are adjunctive, not alternative, in this risk band.
key_points: RANZCOG VTE risk scoring drives dose and duration
Enoxaparin 40 mg daily for 6 weeks if multiple intermediate factors
Mechanical prophylaxis adjunctive, not alternative
Weight-banded dosing in BMI >40 or weight >90 kg
explanation.pearls: Heterozygous factor V Leiden alone does NOT trigger postpartum LMWH, but combined with caesarean, BMI >30, and family history it crosses the threshold for 6 weeks of prophylactic enoxaparin.

sources.label: RANZCOG Venous Thromboembolism in Pregnancy and Postpartum

stem:
You are the antenatal day-assessment registrar reviewing a 29-year-old G2P1 woman at 36+6 weeks gestation who has been referred from her shared-care GP for a breech presentation noted clinically and confirmed on a presentation scan today. She had a normal vaginal birth at term with her first pregnancy two years ago.

This pregnancy has been uncomplicated. Symphysis fundal height is 36 cm, the presenting part is high, and CTG today is normal. The growth scan two weeks ago showed an estimated foetal weight on the 55th centile, amniotic fluid index 14 cm, and the placenta was reported as fundo-anterior with no praevia. Today's presentation scan confirms a frank breech with extended legs and re-states the placenta as fundo-anterior. There is no rupture of membranes, no antepartum haemorrhage and no history of previous uterine surgery. She is keen to attempt a vaginal birth and asks about turning the baby.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 82/min, BP 118/72 mmHg, RR 16/min, SpO2 99% room air, temperature 36.7 °C, foetal heart 142/min
data_table.Examination: SFH 36 cm; presenting part high; uterus soft and non tender; no PV bleeding; no rupture of membranes
data_table.Investigations: Presentation scan: frank breech with extended legs; AFI 14 cm; placenta fundo-anterior; no praevia

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Defer all action and rescan at 38 weeks to allow possible spontaneous version to occur
options.rationale: Spontaneous version after 36 weeks is uncommon (approximately 5%); ECV should be offered at 37 weeks rather than waiting passively. (Source: RANZCOG External Cephalic Version)

options.text: Recommend elective caesarean section at 39 weeks without first offering external cephalic version
options.rationale: Skipping the offer of ECV deprives her of a chance at vaginal birth; routine ECV before elective caesarean is standard RANZCOG practice. (Source: RANZCOG External Cephalic Version)

options.text: Plan a trial of vaginal breech birth at term with continuous CTG monitoring as first option
options.rationale: Planned vaginal breech is permissible after counselling about increased perinatal mortality and morbidity but is offered after ECV failure or refusal, not as first option. (Source: RANZCOG External Cephalic Version)

options.text: Refer for moxibustion at acupuncture point BL67 daily for 14 days before any further decision
options.rationale: Moxibustion has weak supporting evidence; it can be offered as an adjunct but does not substitute for the ECV discussion. (Source: RANZCOG External Cephalic Version)

options.text: Schedule external cephalic version at 37 weeks with terbutaline tocolysis and post-procedure CTG
options.rationale: She is an excellent ECV candidate with no contraindications (uncomplicated singleton, frank breech, normal AFI, fundo-anterior placenta, no scar, no praevia); RANZCOG recommends offering ECV from 37 weeks with tocolysis improving success rates to approximately 60%. (Source: RANZCOG External Cephalic Version)

explanation.summary: External cephalic version is offered from 37 weeks for uncomplicated breech presentations. Success rates are approximately 50% nulliparous and 60% multiparous, improved with tocolysis (salbutamol or terbutaline). Absolute contraindications are placenta praevia, antepartum haemorrhage, ruptured membranes, multiple pregnancy (except second twin), foetal compromise, major foetal anomaly and a previous classical caesarean. Relative contraindications include oligohydramnios, prior caesarean (success and safety similar to no scar in observational data, but discussion required) and small for gestational age. Anti-D is given to Rh negative women. Pre and post procedure CTG is mandatory and immediate caesarean must be available.
key_points: Offer ECV from 37 weeks for uncomplicated breech
Tocolysis improves success (terbutaline or salbutamol)
Anti-D for Rh negative women
Absolute contraindications: praevia, APH, ROM, foetal compromise
explanation.pearls: ECV success is around 60% with multiparity, normal liquor and a posterior or fundo-anterior (not anterior) placenta; tocolysis with terbutaline 250 mcg subcutaneous lifts success by approximately 10 percentage points.

stem:
You are the obstetric registrar in the high-risk antenatal clinic reviewing a 31-year-old G3P1 woman at 14+0 weeks gestation. Her first pregnancy ended in a painless second-trimester loss at 22 weeks following spontaneous rupture of membranes preceded by silent cervical dilation; her second pregnancy ended in a similar loss at 20 weeks. Neither loss had infective features and both placentas showed no chorioamnionitis on histology. She has no other gynaecological history and her first-trimester anatomy and screening have been low risk.

A transvaginal ultrasound today shows a closed external os with a cervical length of 28 mm and no funnelling. Her current pregnancy is singleton, the foetus is structurally normal at the 12-week morphology scan, and the placenta is fundal. She has no vaginal bleeding, no abdominal pain, and no signs of preterm labour. There is no history of cervical surgery or LLETZ. She asks what can be done to reduce the chance of losing this pregnancy.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 78/min, BP 110/68 mmHg, RR 16/min, SpO2 99% room air, temperature 36.7 °C, foetal heart 158/min
data_table.Examination: Closed external os on speculum; no PV bleeding; uterus appropriate for gestation
data_table.Investigations: Transvaginal ultrasound: cervical length 28 mm, no funnelling; singleton; structural anatomy normal

lead_in: What is the most appropriate intervention?

options.text: History indicated cerclage now plus daily vaginal progesterone 200 mg from 16 weeks
options.rationale: Two or more consecutive painless second-trimester losses meet RANZCOG criteria for history-indicated (prophylactic) cerclage placed at 12 to 14 weeks; vaginal progesterone is an evidence-supported adjunct for preterm birth prevention. (Source: RANZCOG Cervical Insufficiency; SMFM Cerclage Guideline)

options.text: Serial transvaginal cervical length scans every 2 weeks and intervene only if shortening
options.rationale: Surveillance is appropriate when the history is equivocal or there has been one prior loss; with two consecutive painless second-trimester losses, history-indicated cerclage is preferred over waiting for sonographic shortening. (Source: SMFM Cerclage Guideline)

options.text: Bed rest at home from 16 weeks until 36 weeks with avoidance of intercourse
options.rationale: Bed rest has no proven benefit in preventing preterm birth and increases maternal VTE and deconditioning risks; it is not a substitute for cerclage where indicated. (Source: RANZCOG Cervical Insufficiency)

options.text: Place an abdominal cerclage by laparoscopy at 12 weeks given two previous failures
options.rationale: Abdominal cerclage is reserved for failed transvaginal cerclage or absent vaginal cervix; first attempt should be a McDonald or Shirodkar transvaginal procedure. (Source: RANZCOG Cervical Insufficiency)

options.text: Administer betamethasone 11.4 mg IM weekly from 24 weeks prophylactically
options.rationale: Antenatal corticosteroids are not given prophylactically by schedule; they are administered when preterm birth is anticipated within 7 days and a single rescue dose at most. (Source: RANZCOG Antenatal Corticosteroids)

explanation.summary: Cervical insufficiency presents as painless, progressive second-trimester cervical dilation often with bulging membranes and is diagnosed by history when there are two or more consecutive second-trimester losses without other cause. Management options are history-indicated cerclage (12 to 14 weeks, recommended for >=2 prior losses), ultrasound-indicated cerclage (offered if cervical length falls below 25 mm before 24 weeks with one prior preterm birth), and physical-examination-indicated (rescue) cerclage for asymptomatic cervical dilation. Vaginal progesterone 200 mg daily from 16 weeks reduces preterm birth in women with a short cervix or prior preterm birth and complements cerclage. McDonald cerclage is the most common technique; Shirodkar and abdominal cerclage are reserved for selected cases.
key_points: >=2 consecutive painless second trimester losses = history-indicated cerclage at 12-14 weeks
Vaginal progesterone 200 mg daily from 16 weeks is adjunctive
Ultrasound-indicated cerclage if cervix <25 mm before 24 weeks with prior preterm
Bed rest has no proven benefit and harms maternal health
explanation.pearls: History-indicated cerclage at 12 to 14 weeks plus daily vaginal progesterone 200 mg from 16 weeks is the RANZCOG recommended package for women with two or more consecutive painless second-trimester losses.

stem:
You are the on-call obstetric registrar reviewing a 26-year-old G1P0 woman in active labour at 39+4 weeks gestation. Her membranes ruptured 22 hours ago and she has had spontaneous progress to 5 cm dilation with regular contractions. She had a prolonged second stage planned and is on an epidural. Her group B streptococcus swab at 36 weeks was negative.

Over the last hour her temperature has risen from 36.9 °C to 38.4 °C, recorded by the midwife on two readings 20 minutes apart. Her maternal heart rate is 118/min. Foetal CTG shows a baseline of 168/min with reduced variability and no decelerations. The amniotic fluid drained at vaginal examination is cloudy and offensive. She is otherwise stable, with a normal blood pressure and respiratory rate, and is comfortable on epidural with no abdominal tenderness between contractions. She is not allergic to penicillin or to any other medication.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 118/min, BP 122/72 mmHg, RR 18/min, SpO2 99% room air, temperature 38.4 °C, foetal heart 168/min baseline
data_table.Examination: 5 cm dilation; cloudy offensive amniotic fluid; no abdominal tenderness; epidural in situ
data_table.Investigations: Maternal CTG baseline 168/min reduced variability no decelerations; WCC pending; GBS swab negative at 36 weeks

lead_in: What is the most appropriate antibiotic regimen?

options.text: Withhold antibiotics until blood cultures return and target therapy then
options.rationale: Empirical broad spectrum cover must start immediately when chorioamnionitis is diagnosed; waiting for cultures risks neonatal sepsis. (Source: RANZCOG Intrapartum Fever)

options.text: Intravenous benzylpenicillin 1.2 g 4-hourly alone for GBS coverage
options.rationale: GBS monotherapy is appropriate intrapartum prophylaxis with positive screen, NOT chorioamnionitis treatment; once infection is established, broader gram-negative cover is needed. (Source: RANZCOG Intrapartum Fever)

options.text: Intravenous ceftriaxone 1 g daily plus metronidazole 500 mg 12-hourly
options.rationale: Ceftriaxone-metronidazole is an alternative for severe penicillin allergy, but is not the standard first-line regimen in Australia which uses ampicillin plus gentamicin. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Antibiotic)

options.text: Oral cephalexin 500 mg 6-hourly plus expectant management of labour
options.rationale: Oral antibiotics are inadequate for established intrapartum infection; IV therapy is required and continued until 24 hours post-delivery (or per institutional protocol). (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Antibiotic)

options.text: Intravenous ampicillin 2 g 6-hourly plus gentamicin 5 mg/kg daily
options.rationale: Intrapartum chorioamnionitis empiric therapy in Australia is IV ampicillin 2 g 6-hourly plus gentamicin 5 mg/kg daily; this covers ascending genital tract organisms including GBS, E. coli and Enterococcus. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Antibiotic; RANZCOG Intrapartum Fever)

explanation.summary: Clinical chorioamnionitis is diagnosed by maternal fever >=38 °C plus one of foetal tachycardia, maternal tachycardia, uterine tenderness or purulent amniotic fluid. Empirical IV antibiotics target ascending genital flora including GBS, E. coli and Enterococcus; the Australian first-line is ampicillin plus gentamicin. Add clindamycin or metronidazole if caesarean delivery occurs to cover anaerobes. Delivery is the definitive treatment; antibiotics typically continue for 24 hours postpartum, longer if features of sepsis persist. The neonatal team should attend delivery given increased early-onset sepsis risk.
key_points: Fever >=38 °C plus one of foetal/maternal tachycardia, uterine tenderness, purulent liquor
Empirical IV ampicillin 2 g 6h plus gentamicin 5 mg/kg daily
Add clindamycin or metronidazole if caesarean (anaerobe cover)
Delivery is definitive; neonatal team to attend
explanation.pearls: Australian first-line for clinical chorioamnionitis is ampicillin 2 g IV 6-hourly plus gentamicin 5 mg/kg IV daily; ceftriaxone-metronidazole is the alternative for severe penicillin allergy, not first-line.

stem:
You are the senior obstetric registrar at a tertiary delivery suite called urgently to a 34-year-old G4P3 woman who collapsed two minutes after the spontaneous vaginal birth of a 4.2 kg male infant at 41+2 weeks. The third stage had been managed actively with intramuscular oxytocin 10 IU. The placenta is in situ.

While you arrive she becomes unresponsive, has a witnessed seizure, and stops breathing. Pulse is impalpable centrally. Cardiopulmonary resuscitation is commenced immediately. The midwife reports she became suddenly breathless, said she felt cold, and complained of impending doom moments before losing consciousness. There was a small amount of pink frothy sputum at her mouth. She has no past history of asthma or epilepsy, did not receive epidural, and a peripheral cannula sited 30 minutes ago for hydration has been kept patent. The first bloods drawn during resuscitation show a fibrinogen of 0.8 g/L (1.5-4.0), platelets 78 x10^9/L, INR 2.4 and pH 6.98.

data_table.Vital signs: No central pulse; previously: pulse 142/min, BP 70/40 mmHg, SpO2 unrecordable, RR 0 (apnoeic)
data_table.Examination: Unresponsive after seizure; pink frothy sputum at mouth; uterus tone unclear with placenta in situ; no obvious haemorrhage source
data_table.Investigations: Fibrinogen 0.8 g/L (1.5-4.0); platelets 78 x10^9/L; INR 2.4; pH 6.98; lactate 9.2 mmol/L

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Defer resuscitation and arrange urgent CT pulmonary angiogram to confirm pulmonary embolism
options.rationale: A peri-arrest collapse cannot wait for imaging; treatment is empiric supportive ALS with MTP, and CTPA is not part of acute AFE management. (Source: ANZCOR ALS)

options.text: Give intravenous adrenaline 100 mcg boluses for presumed anaphylaxis to active third stage oxytocin
options.rationale: Oxytocin anaphylaxis is extremely rare and does NOT explain the DIC; treating AFE as anaphylaxis delays appropriate resuscitation and coagulopathy correction. (Source: RANZCOG Amniotic Fluid Embolism)

options.text: Administer intravenous magnesium sulfate 4 g for presumed eclampsia and continue observation
options.rationale: Without preceding pre-eclampsia or hypertension, eclampsia is unlikely; the DIC and pink frothy sputum point to AFE, and magnesium does not address the haemodynamic collapse. (Source: SOMANZ Hypertension in Pregnancy)

options.text: Continue CPR, secure airway, activate massive transfusion protocol, escalate to obstetric and ICU consultants
options.rationale: The collapse plus seizure plus DIC profile is amniotic fluid embolism until proven otherwise; management is supportive ALS plus aggressive correction of coagulopathy with MTP, with team-based escalation. (Source: ANZCOR ALS; RANZCOG Amniotic Fluid Embolism; SOMANZ AFE)

options.text: Start tranexamic acid 1 g intravenously and observe response before further escalation
options.rationale: TXA is given as part of PPH management but is not a substitute for full massive transfusion and ALS in cardiovascular collapse with established DIC. (Source: RANZCOG Postpartum Haemorrhage)

explanation.summary: Amniotic fluid embolism is a catastrophic obstetric event characterised by sudden cardiorespiratory collapse, hypoxia, neurological compromise (seizure or altered consciousness) and disseminated intravascular coagulation, usually around delivery or in the immediate postpartum period. The clinical picture - obstetric peripartum timing plus collapse plus DIC - is itself the diagnosis; no laboratory test is pathognomonic. Management is bundles-based: ANZCOR ALS for cardiac arrest, early airway control, supportive ventilation, aggressive correction of coagulopathy with massive transfusion protocol (red cells, FFP, cryoprecipitate, platelets), fibrinogen replacement, and consideration of veno-arterial ECMO at tertiary centres. Mortality remains 20-60%. Documentation and structured debriefing are mandatory.
key_points: Diagnosis is clinical: peripartum collapse + DIC + hypoxia +/- seizure
ANZCOR ALS plus airway plus MTP plus team escalation
Fibrinogen <2 g/L is a strong AFE marker, replace with cryoprecipitate
Consider VA-ECMO at tertiary centres
explanation.pearls: AFE often presents in two phases: an initial pulmonary hypertensive/cardiogenic shock phase from amniotic debris in the pulmonary vasculature, followed within an hour by florid DIC; fibrinogen below 2 g/L is the strongest single discriminator from haemorrhagic shock with secondary coagulopathy.

stem:
You are the consultation-liaison psychiatry registrar reviewing a 32-year-old woman with bipolar I disorder who has been stable on sodium valproate 1000 mg daily for the last 3 years. She has had no manic or depressive episodes since starting valproate. Before this she had two hospital admissions for mania, both requiring inpatient stabilisation. She is in a stable relationship and her partner is also her primary carer.

She attends today to tell you that she and her partner are planning to start a family and would like to try to conceive within the next 6 months. She is using the combined oral contraceptive pill. Her latest valproate level is 78 mg/L (50-100), full blood count, liver function and weight are within normal limits, and she has had no significant tremor or hair thinning. She takes no other regular medication. Her older sister has bipolar I and conceived on lithium without complications.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 72/min, BP 118/72 mmHg, RR 14/min, SpO2 99% room air, temperature 36.7 °C
data_table.Examination: Euthymic; affect appropriate; no thought disorder; no psychomotor abnormality
data_table.Investigations: Valproate level 78 mg/L (50-100); FBC, LFT within normal limits; BHCG negative; weight stable

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Continue valproate at the same dose but add folic acid 5 mg daily from preconception
options.rationale: Folic acid 5 mg reduces neural tube defect risk modestly but does not address the broader spectrum of valproate teratogenicity; continuing valproate when alternatives exist breaches the TGA pregnancy prevention programme. (Source: TGA Valproate Pregnancy Prevention Programme)

options.text: Switch from sodium valproate to lithium with cross titration over 6 to 8 weeks before conception
options.rationale: Sodium valproate is teratogenic (10% major malformations, 40% neurodevelopmental impairment) and is contraindicated in women planning pregnancy unless no alternative exists; the RANZCP and TGA pregnancy prevention programme mandates discontinuation before conception, with lithium an evidence-supported alternative given prior good response in the family. (Source: RANZCP Bipolar Guidelines; TGA Valproate Pregnancy Prevention Programme; AMH)

options.text: Reduce valproate to 500 mg daily to lower exposure and continue through pregnancy
options.rationale: Lower-dose valproate retains teratogenicity (especially neurodevelopmental risk above 700 mg/day) and would also raise relapse risk; dose reduction is not a safe pregnancy strategy. (Source: TGA Valproate Pregnancy Prevention Programme)

options.text: Switch to lamotrigine 200 mg daily as a mood stabiliser before conception planning
options.rationale: Lamotrigine has lower teratogenicity than valproate but is more effective for bipolar depression than for mania prevention; given her prior manic episodes lithium is the better-evidenced choice. (Source: RANZCP Bipolar Guidelines)

options.text: Stop valproate immediately with no alternative pharmacotherapy and offer psychological therapy alone
options.rationale: Abrupt discontinuation without bridging mood stabiliser carries a high mania relapse risk; supervised cross-titration to a safer agent is the correct approach. (Source: RANZCP Bipolar Guidelines)

explanation.summary: Sodium valproate is the most teratogenic of all anticonvulsants and mood stabilisers, with a 10% major malformation rate (neural tube defects, cardiac, craniofacial, limb anomalies) and a 30 to 40% neurodevelopmental impairment rate; both rates are dose-dependent above 700 mg/day. The TGA pregnancy prevention programme (mirroring the European RMP) requires that women of reproductive potential do not receive valproate unless no alternative is effective, that they use highly effective contraception, and that annual specialist review confirms ongoing need. Cross-titration to lithium, lamotrigine or an atypical antipsychotic is the standard pre-conception plan. Lithium carries a small risk of Ebstein's anomaly (now estimated at <1%) which is acceptable against valproate exposure.
key_points: Valproate teratogenicity: 10% malformation, 30-40% neurodevelopmental
TGA Valproate Pregnancy Prevention Programme mandates alternatives
Lithium acceptable in bipolar I planning pregnancy (Ebstein risk <1%)
Cross-titrate before conception; do not stop abruptly
explanation.pearls: Valproate confers a 30-40% neurodevelopmental impairment risk (foetal valproate syndrome) and a 10% major malformation rate; lithium's Ebstein's anomaly risk in pregnancy is now estimated at under 1%, making lithium the preferred swap for bipolar I planning conception.

stem:
You are the ED registrar at a metropolitan tertiary hospital reviewing a 24-year-old woman brought in by ambulance one hour after an intentional ingestion of approximately 30 tablets of amitriptyline 50 mg, taken with vodka. She was found by her partner who called triple-0 immediately. She had been taking amitriptyline for migraine prophylaxis. She has no other regular medications and no significant past medical history.

On arrival she is drowsy but rousable to voice. Pupils are 6 mm and sluggish. She has dry warm skin and absent bowel sounds. Her airway is patent but she cannot protect it if any further deterioration occurs. The ECG performed on arrival shows sinus rhythm at 118/min with a QRS duration of 132 ms and a terminal R wave in aVR of 4 mm; the QTc is 480 ms. Venous blood gas shows pH 7.28, base excess -8. She is currently haemodynamically stable with a blood pressure of 102/64 mmHg but the ECG was repeated 10 minutes later and the QRS is now 142 ms.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 118/min, BP 102/64 mmHg, RR 14/min, SpO2 96% room air, temperature 37.4 °C
data_table.Examination: GCS 12 (E3 V4 M5); pupils 6 mm sluggish; dry warm skin; absent bowel sounds; airway patent
data_table.Investigations: ECG: QRS 132 ms then 142 ms over 10 min; aVR terminal R 4 mm; QTc 480 ms; pH 7.28; base excess -8

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Administer intravenous flumazenil 0.2 mg for CNS depression from any co-ingested benzodiazepine
options.rationale: Flumazenil is contraindicated in mixed overdose with sodium channel blockade because it can precipitate seizures that worsen acidosis and TCA toxicity. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Toxicology)

options.text: Administer activated charcoal 1 g/kg via nasogastric tube and observe with cardiac monitoring
options.rationale: Charcoal at one hour post-ingestion may have some role in alert patients with intact airway, but in a drowsy patient with progressive QRS it should not delay sodium bicarbonate or airway control. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Toxicology)

options.text: Commence intravenous magnesium sulfate 2 g for torsades prophylaxis given QTc 480 ms
options.rationale: Torsades is uncommon in TCA overdose; the dominant arrhythmia mechanism is sodium channel blockade, addressed by bicarbonate. Magnesium is reserved for documented torsades or hypomagnesaemia. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Toxicology)

options.text: Administer intravenous sodium bicarbonate 1 to 2 mmol/kg and prepare for definitive airway control
options.rationale: QRS prolongation >100 ms with widening trend in TCA overdose is the trigger for IV sodium bicarbonate 1 to 2 mmol/kg bolus to overcome cardiac sodium channel blockade; ongoing CNS depression with rising QRS heralds seizure and arrhythmia and warrants airway control. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Toxicology; ANZCOR Toxic Cardiac Arrest)

options.text: Defer all therapy until a serum amitriptyline level returns to guide management severity
options.rationale: TCA levels do not correlate well with toxicity and are not used to guide acute management; ECG and clinical features drive treatment. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Toxicology)

explanation.summary: Tricyclic antidepressant overdose carries cardiotoxicity (sodium channel blockade causing QRS widening, hypotension, ventricular arrhythmia), CNS depression (drowsiness, seizures), anticholinergic effects (dry mouth, mydriasis, urinary retention, tachycardia) and alpha-blockade (hypotension). ECG markers of severe toxicity are QRS >100 ms, terminal R wave in aVR >3 mm, and rightward axis. Sodium bicarbonate 1 to 2 mmol/kg IV is the antidote, working by alkalinisation and sodium loading to displace TCA from cardiac sodium channels; aim for arterial pH 7.50 to 7.55. Hypertonic saline is an alternative when alkalosis is contraindicated. Lipid emulsion is reserved for refractory arrest. Intubation is required for GCS deterioration, seizures or progressive cardiotoxicity to prevent acidaemia worsening sodium channel blockade.
key_points: QRS >100 ms or aVR terminal R >3 mm = severe toxicity
IV NaHCO3 1-2 mmol/kg bolus targeting pH 7.50-7.55
Intubate for progressive CNS depression to prevent acidaemic deterioration
Flumazenil contraindicated in TCA co-ingestion
explanation.pearls: In tricyclic overdose, QRS over 100 ms predicts seizures and QRS over 160 ms predicts ventricular arrhythmia; IV sodium bicarbonate 1 to 2 mmol/kg targets QRS narrowing and arterial pH 7.50 to 7.55.

stem:
You are the on-call psychiatry registrar reviewing a 15-year-old girl in the ED who was brought in by her mother after she discovered superficial cuts to her forearm. The cuts were made with a kitchen knife two hours ago, are 1 to 2 mm deep, did not require sutures, and the mother is now waiting in the cubicle. She is in Year 10 at a local high school and is normally a good student.

She is reluctant to talk in front of her mother. When asked privately she discloses she has been cutting for the past three months as a way to cope with overwhelming feelings since her parents separated. She denies any current plans for suicide and does not have a plan or means in mind. She has had occasional fleeting thoughts that life is not worth living when very distressed but has not made any preparations. She has not used any substances. She does not own or have access to firearms. She is willing to engage with follow up and her mother is supportive.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 78/min, BP 110/68 mmHg, RR 14/min, SpO2 99% room air, temperature 36.7 °C
data_table.Mental state: Tearful but cooperative; affect reactive; thought form linear; no psychotic features; denies current suicidal plan or intent
data_table.Examination: Superficial cuts left forearm 1-2 mm deep, healing primarily, no signs of infection; no other injuries

lead_in: What is the most appropriate disposition?

options.text: Discharge home with safety net advice only and a GP letter for review in 6 weeks
options.rationale: Six week GP review is too long for a first NSSI presentation; structured safety planning plus rapid CAMHS follow up within 72 hours is the standard. (Source: RANZCP CPG: Adolescent Mood and Self Harm)
source_refs: RANZCP CPG: Adolescent Mood and Self Harm

options.text: Involuntary admission to an adolescent inpatient mental health unit under the Mental Health Act
options.rationale: Involuntary admission requires risk of serious harm to self or others not manageable in the community; her chronic NSSI without current intent or plan can be safely managed outpatient with parental support. (Source: RANZCP CPG: Adolescent Mood and Self Harm)
source_refs: RANZCP CPG: Adolescent Mood and Self Harm

options.text: Voluntary admission to a paediatric ward for 48 hours of observation and family meetings
options.rationale: Brief paediatric ward admission for chronic NSSI lacks therapeutic structure, can reinforce admission-seeking and is not recommended; outpatient follow up is preferred when risk allows. (Source: RANZCP CPG: Adolescent Mood and Self Harm)
source_refs: RANZCP CPG: Adolescent Mood and Self Harm

options.text: Commence fluoxetine 20 mg orally daily and arrange outpatient follow up in two weeks
options.rationale: Pharmacotherapy is not first-line for adolescent self-harm without a clear major depressive episode; psychological intervention (DBT-informed or CBT) is the first step. (Source: RANZCP CPG: Adolescent Mood and Self Harm)
source_refs: RANZCP CPG: Adolescent Mood and Self Harm

options.text: Discharge home with her mother, a safety plan, and CAMHS outpatient follow up within 72 hours
options.rationale: She has chronic non-suicidal self-injury without current suicidal intent or plan, a supportive parent, and willingness to engage; current Australian guidelines support outpatient management with structured safety planning and rapid CAMHS follow up. (Source: RANZCP CPG: Adolescent Mood and Self Harm; eMHPrac)
source_refs: RANZCP CPG: Adolescent Mood and Self Harm

explanation.summary: Adolescent non-suicidal self-injury is common (lifetime prevalence around 17%) and most often reflects emotion regulation rather than suicidal intent, but it is a marker of future suicide risk and warrants structured assessment. The HEEADSSS framework guides psychosocial history. Safety planning is collaboratively constructed with the young person and a parent or trusted adult: it lists warning signs, internal coping strategies, social distractions, people to contact, professional supports and means restriction. Rapid follow up with the local Child and Adolescent Mental Health Service (CAMHS) within 72 hours is the Australian standard. Inpatient admission is reserved for acute suicidal intent with means or plan, severe psychiatric illness needing inpatient treatment, or unsafe home environment.
key_points: NSSI lifetime prevalence in adolescents around 17%
Structured safety plan with means restriction is core
CAMHS rapid follow up within 72 hours
Inpatient admission only for active suicidal intent with plan/means or unsafe home
explanation.pearls: Safety planning in adolescent self-harm follows the Stanley-Brown template: warning signs, internal coping, social distractions, support people, professional contacts and means restriction; rapid CAMHS follow up within 72 hours is the Australian discharge standard.

sources.label: RANZCP CPG: Adolescent Mood and Self Harm

stem:
You are the consultation-liaison psychiatry registrar reviewing a 68-year-old woman on the general medical ward referred for unresponsiveness for the last 48 hours. She has a 10 year history of recurrent major depression with two previous hospitalisations, currently on mirtazapine 30 mg nocte. Two weeks ago her GP added quetiapine 50 mg nocte for sleep and ruminations. She lives alone and her daughter brought her to the ED when she found her on the couch in the same position from the previous day.

On examination she is mute, eyes open but unblinking, lying immobile in bed. She does not respond to verbal stimuli. When you raise her arm above her head it remains in position for over a minute (waxy flexibility, positive Bush Francis catatonia rating). The medical team have excluded infection (clear CXR, normal urine, normal CRP), electrolyte derangement (sodium 138, calcium 2.32), thyroid disease (TSH normal), and a non contrast CT head and CT venogram are within normal limits. She has no fever and is haemodynamically stable.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 88/min, BP 132/82 mmHg, RR 14/min, SpO2 99% room air, temperature 36.7 °C
data_table.Examination: Mute, immobile, waxy flexibility positive; Bush Francis Catatonia Rating Scale 14; no fever; no rigidity; no tremor
data_table.Investigations: Sodium 138 mmol/L; calcium 2.32 mmol/L; TSH within normal limits; CRP <5 mg/L; CT head and venogram within normal limits

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Cease mirtazapine immediately and start fluoxetine 20 mg as antidepressant rotation
options.rationale: Antidepressant rotation does not address acute catatonia; benzodiazepines or ECT are the treatments, and abrupt cessation has its own risks. (Source: RANZCP CPG Catatonia)

options.text: Administer intravenous lorazepam 1 to 2 mg challenge and reassess in 10 minutes
options.rationale: Bush Francis positive catatonia in the absence of fever and rigidity is non-malignant catatonia; first-line treatment is a lorazepam challenge 1 to 2 mg IV, with response (often dramatic) confirming diagnosis and guiding maintenance dosing of 6 to 24 mg/day. (Source: RANZCP CPG Catatonia; AMH)

options.text: Start dantrolene 1 mg/kg IV given suspected neuroleptic malignant syndrome from quetiapine
options.rationale: There is no fever, no rigidity and no autonomic instability; NMS is not the syndrome, and dantrolene exposes her to hepatotoxicity without benefit. (Source: RANZCP CPG Catatonia)

options.text: Arrange urgent EEG to exclude non-convulsive status epilepticus before any treatment
options.rationale: Non-convulsive status is worth excluding when investigations are equivocal, but should not delay a lorazepam challenge which is itself anticonvulsant and diagnostic. (Source: RANZCP CPG Catatonia)

options.text: Refer immediately for electroconvulsive therapy with 3-times-weekly bilateral sessions
options.rationale: ECT is highly effective and is the second-line treatment if lorazepam fails; in current Australian practice the lorazepam challenge is offered first because of its diagnostic yield and lower invasiveness. (Source: RANZCP CPG Catatonia)

explanation.summary: Catatonia is a syndrome of motor immobility, mutism, posturing, waxy flexibility, negativism, echolalia, echopraxia and stereotypies that may arise in mood disorders, schizophrenia, autism, post-stroke states, drug-induced presentations and medical illness. The Bush Francis Catatonia Rating Scale identifies cases and malignant catatonia adds fever, rigidity, autonomic instability and elevated CK. The lorazepam challenge (1 to 2 mg IV) is both diagnostic and therapeutic with response within 10 minutes confirming the syndrome. Maintenance lorazepam 6 to 24 mg/day is titrated to response and ECT is the second-line treatment reserved for non-responders or malignant catatonia. Antipsychotics should be paused as they can worsen non-malignant catatonia.
key_points: Bush Francis Catatonia Rating Scale for diagnosis
IV lorazepam 1-2 mg challenge is first-line; maintenance 6-24 mg/day
Pause antipsychotics during catatonia treatment
Malignant catatonia (fever, rigidity, autonomic instability, raised CK) needs ECT
explanation.pearls: The lorazepam challenge in catatonia: 1 to 2 mg IV with reassessment at 10 minutes; a dramatic response (mute patient who suddenly speaks, eats, moves) is both diagnostic and guides maintenance titration to 6 to 24 mg/day.
